concepts.lesson: Light is a form of energy that enables us to see objects. Sources of light can be natural (like the Sun) or artificial (like bulbs and candles). Understanding light sources is fundamental for studying shadows and reflections.
concepts.real_example: The Sun provides daylight, while a flashlight lights up a dark room.

concepts.lesson: A shadow is formed when an opaque object blocks the path of light. The size and shape of the shadow depend on the position of the object relative to the light source. Shadows demonstrate that light travels in straight lines.
concepts.real_example: A tree casts a shadow on the ground when sunlight falls on it.
key_points: Shadow forms when light is blocked
Depends on object position and light source
Shows light travels in straight lines

concepts.lesson: Reflection is the bouncing back of light from a surface. Smooth surfaces like mirrors produce regular reflection, forming clear images, while rough surfaces produce diffused reflection. Understanding reflection is essential for mirrors and optical devices.
concepts.real_example: Seeing your face in a bathroom mirror is due to reflection.

concepts.lesson: The laws of reflection are: (1) The incident ray, reflected ray, and normal all lie in the same plane, and (2) The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection. These laws help in predicting the path of reflected light.
concepts.real_example: Laser light reflecting off a flat mirror follows the angle-of-incidence = angle-of-reflection rule.
key_points: Incident ray, reflected ray, and normal are coplanar
Angle of incidence = angle of reflection

concepts.lesson: A plane mirror forms virtual, erect, and laterally inverted images of objects. The size of the image is equal to the size of the object. Understanding plane mirrors is important for daily life and optical applications.
concepts.real_example: Seeing yourself in a dressing mirror forms a virtual image behind the mirror.
key_points: Virtual, erect, and laterally inverted images

concepts.lesson: Spherical mirrors are curved mirrors: concave (converging) and convex (diverging). They form real or virtual images depending on object position. Concave mirrors can magnify or focus light, while convex mirrors provide a wider field of view.
concepts.real_example: Shaving mirrors are concave, while vehicle rear-view mirrors are convex.
key_points: Used in telescopes, mirrors, and vehicles

concepts.lesson: Refraction is the bending of light when it passes from one medium to another (like air to water). Refraction explains phenomena such as the apparent bending of a straw in water and is important in lens-based devices.
concepts.real_example: A straight stick appears bent when partially immersed in water due to refraction.
key_points: Bending of light at interface of two media
Explains lenses, glasses, and optical devices

concepts.concept_name: Formation of Shadows with Multiple Light Sources
concepts.lesson: When multiple light sources illuminate an object, multiple shadows are formed. The position, number, and intensity of shadows depend on the relative position of sources. This concept explains penumbra and umbra formation.
concepts.real_example: Streetlights create overlapping shadows of a tree at night.
key_points: Multiple shadows from multiple light sources
